retrieved_docs.title: Ventriculostomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: There have been no significant differences observed in terms of the risk of infection or complications while comparing the EVDs placed either in the operating room or at the bedside. The experience of the surgeons also did not influence the outcome. [1] [23] The use of bolt EVDs has minimal risks of CSF leak and tube migration. [3] Absolute risk reduction of around 20% has been reported by applying the antibiotic-coated catheter. [2] The anatomical point of ventriculostomy has not been shown to alter the risk of infection. [2] Rapid weaning within 24 hours has been advocated rather than the training of the EVD (raising the height of the drain until clamping). [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Ventriculostomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Strict adherence to EVD care bundles is of paramount importance in reducing the risk of infection. The most rational strategy is to avoid its insertion whenever possible and implement it only when deemed necessary. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Ventriculoperitoneal Shunt -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the management considerations for patients with a VP shunt. Outline the typical presentation of a patient with a VP shunt malfunction. Recall the complications associated with the placement of a VP shunt. Outline the importance of improving care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients undergoing insertion of a VP shunt. retrieved_docs.title: Ventriculoperitoneal Shunt -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Ventriculoperitoneal shunts are used to treat hydrocephalus and divert CSF from the lateral ventricles into the peritoneum. Tapping a shunt is performed for both diagnostic and therapeutic reasons (evaluate for infection or malfunction). A VP shunt is one of the high impact advances made in neurosurgical patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: Ventriculostomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Various clinical parameters shown to harbinger the risk of infection include: CSF leak Frequent CSF sampling (each sampling increasing the risk by 8.3%) Increased duration of catheter insertion (9.3% with time duration of ≥8 days) Frequent changing of the catheter (each increasing the risk by 4-fold) The dictum of catheter irrigation. [2] [20] [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is recognized as a neurological disorder that involves the brain and nerves, and it is specifically identified within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway where excess cerebrospinal fluid accumulates. Clinically, hydrocephalus is associated with the development of dementia (خرف). In the management of hydrocephalus, shunting procedures are commonly employed; the ventricular‑peritoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) frequently co‑occurs with the condition and represents a specific type of shunt that diverts fluid from a cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site. This shunt is a child concept of the broader category “shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is nested under the procedural domain of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts. Another variant, the ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx, is performed directly on the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway and is considered closely related, and possibly synonymous, with the general insertion/replacement/removal procedure for extracranial ventricular shunts. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose hydrocephalus, anticipate its cognitive complications, and select appropriate shunting interventions for optimal patient care.

This type of shunt diverts cerebrospinal fluid from the brain’s ventricles to an extracranial location, typically the peritoneal cavity, and is employed in the management of conditions such as hydrocephalus.

retrieved_docs.content: Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is a neurosurgical procedure that falls under the broader category of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt. Within this category, the specific technique known as التحويلة البطينية البريتونية is identified as a child procedure, representing a particular method of diverting cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to an extracranial location such as the peritoneal cavity. Recognizing that Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is a subset of the general shunt management interventions and that التحويلة البطينية البريتونية is a specific variant of this procedure aids clinicians in selecting and performing the appropriate surgical approach for conditions like hydrocephalus.

The shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is positioned within the broader category of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt and has a child procedure called التحويلة البطينية البريتونية (ventriculoperitoneal shunt). Additionally, the ventricular shunt to nasopharynx is related to, and possibly synonymous with, the general procedure of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 976
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is classified both as a neurological disorder and as a physical‑psychosomatic disorder, reflecting its broad impact on the central nervous system. The condition is identified within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway, which serves as the finding site of استسقاء الدماغ. Clinically, استسقاء الدماغ is associated with senile dementia (خرف شيخوخي), indicating that chronic ventricular enlargement can contribute to cognitive decline in older adults. In practice, استسقاء الدماغ frequently co‑occurs with a ventriculoperitoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية), a surgical device used to divert excess cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to an extracranial location. The ventriculoperitoneal shunt is a child procedure of the broader category “Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is a subtype of “insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt.” One specific operative technique within this hierarchy is the ventricular shunt to nasopharynx, which is described as a direct procedure site of the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway. This ventricular shunt to nasopharynx is considered related and possibly synonymous with the general procedure of insertion, replacement, or removal of an extracranial ventricular shunt. Understanding the interconnections among استسقاء الدماغ, its anatomical location, its clinical link to خرف شيخوخي, and the various shunting procedures—including the ventriculoperitoneal shunt and ventricular shunt to nasopharynx—provides clinicians with a comprehensive framework for diagnosing hydrocephalus, assessing its cognitive consequences, and selecting appropriate surgical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 258
retrieved_docs.content: ventricular shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية), a type of shunt that diverts cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to an extracranial site. Procedurally, the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway is the direct procedure site for a ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx. The broader category of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts is classified as a ventricular‑vascular shunt (تحويلة بطينية وعائية) and falls under the specialty of neurosurgery (جراحة الأعصاب). This procedural category includes both the shunt of a cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site and its specific child procedure, the peritoneal ventricular shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية). The shunt of a cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site is itself a child of the insertion/replacement/removal procedure, and the ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx is considered related and possibly synonymous with that same procedural group. Clinically, استسقاء الدماغ is associated with both خرف (dementia) and خرف شيخوخي (senile dementia), indicating that untreated or chronic hydrocephalus can contribute to cognitive decline. retrieved_docs.content: The brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway serves as the direct procedure site of a ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx. The clinical activity described as insertion, replacement, or removal of an extracranial ventricular shunt is classified under the Arabic term تحويلة بطينية وعائية and functions as the overarching category for shunt procedures that divert cerebral ventricular fluid to extracranial locations. Within this hierarchy, the specific entity “Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site” is a child of the insertion/replacement/removal procedure and itself has a child procedure named التحويلة البطينية البريتونية, which denotes a ventricular‑peritoneal (intracranial‑to‑peritoneal) shunt. The ventricular shunt to nasopharynx is closely related to, and may be synonymous with, the insertion, replacement, or removal of an extracranial ventricular shunt, indicating that these terms refer to the same operative concept. This structured relationship clarifies how various shunt types and their procedural classifications are interconnected in the management of cerebrospinal fluid diversion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: Insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt is a neurosurgical procedural category that is classified as تحويلة بطينية وعائية. This category also functions as its own classifier, defining the scope of procedures that involve the placement, exchange, or extraction of shunts that divert cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to an extracranial location. Within this broader category, the specific procedure Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is identified as a subordinate (child) entity, indicating that it falls under the umbrella of insertion, replacement, or removal activities. Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site further includes the child entity التحويلة البطينية البريتونية, which denotes a ventriculoperitoneal shunt—a common type of extracranial ventricular shunt used to treat hydrocephalus. By mapping these hierarchical relationships, clinicians can understand that التحويلة البطينية البريتونية is a particular instance of a Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site, and both are encompassed by the broader procedural group of Insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt, classified as تحويلة بطينية وعائية. This structured knowledge aids in accurate coding, procedural planning, and communication across multidisciplinary teams caring for patients requiring ventricular shunt management.
